tasks.title: Enable multi-iteration execution per task with refined follow-up prompts
evidence: User request: Add a capability to do multiple iterations per task, keeping the three-phase approach (support 1, 2, and 3 phases) but looping the same task multiple times with refined prompts and per-pass reasoning effort.
plan: Define the iteration model (config defaults, optional per-task override) including how many loops to run and how phases map per loop.
Implement queue/task and config support for iteration counts and per-iteration reasoning effort overrides while keeping backward compatibility explicit.
Update runner loop execution to repeat the task for the configured number of iterations, preserving phase behavior (1, 2, or 3 phases) each time.
Revise prompts for follow-up iterations to focus on refinement and regression review without explicitly stating pass numbers.
Add tests for iteration scheduling, config/task overrides, and prompt selection behavior across loops.
Update CLI/docs to describe iteration configuration, task fields, and usage examples.
tasks.request: Add a capability to do multiple iterations per task. You may ask, why? The reason is that some models are more capable than others. Oftentimes, a dumber model does syntactically correct and functional code, but it does it in a way that is sloppy. So by having that same dumber model run across the same task multiple times, it enables iteration that enhances outcomes. On the initial loop, the agents have to examine the current state, and then come up with the solution and implement the solution for the task at hand. This leads to a lot of context being used on exploration and contemplation. Context gets bloated, and quality decreases because a lot of irrelevant information ends up in the context. On the subsequent passes for the same task, since the task has already been achieved, it has more of a clear context to be able to focus on how to do it better instead of having to come up with the new implementation solution altogether. Iteration enhances outcomes and leads to better outcomes. So for example, keep the three-phased approach, but do two loops on the same task, on the second go-round, it will be prompted that it had already been executed once before, and on the second go-round, it makes sure to identify any unexpected or unplanned behavior changes, poor coding habits. Outstanding follow-ups that need to be executed. And just does overall refinement to make it a better approach. Think of it like multiple senior developers doing like a peer review refinement process per task. This will lead to higher outcomes. Currently, the three-phase approach consists of phase one planning, phase two implementing the plan, and phase three completing any outstanding work that remained after phase two, as well as doing a thorough review for bugs, regressions, etc. Phase three is essentially like a code review, but it can also make changes. We will likely want to tweak the prompts for the second loop, or what. I want to avoid using any verbiage in the second and any subsequent loops that denotes that it is doing a second pass or a third pass, etc., because that may cause the agent to get a little lazy and assume that things are already done. I want to encourage the comprehensive approach liDon't repeat yourself as certainly a principle I want in place. Code readability, code maintainability, concise code, elegantly simple code but not feature or functionally simple code. I think it would be beneficial to be able to have different levels of reasoning applied for the initial pass of the task and the subsequent passes. I could see some potential downsides, but the reason to do this is to fold one to decrease runtime and for cost purposes, but if we have a model run on the first pass with high reasoning, it can spend a lot of time on doing it correctly or mostly correct on that initial pass and then on subsequent runs, since the task is overall a bit easier due to existing solutions being in place, we could have a lower reasoning level configured. We already allow for the reasoning effort to be controlled globally and by project in the config file, as well as have task-specific reasoning set in the task queue.

tasks.title: Add per-task model_effort override for runner reasoning effort
evidence: User request: Add support for a 'model_effort' field in the queue schema with default 'default' that overrides config reasoning_effort per task; support low/medium/high/xhigh and align accepted levels accordingly.
plan: Update the queue task schema and parsing/validation to accept optional model_effort with default handling and supported values (default, low, medium, high, xhigh).
Wire model_effort through runner selection so it overrides config reasoning_effort when the runner/model supports it (codex), and falls back to config when default or absent.
Align reasoning effort enum/validation across config and runners to the codex-supported levels and document any breaking changes.
Add tests for schema validation, config fallback, and runner override behavior for supported/unsupported values.
Update docs (queue-and-tasks, configuration, CLI help if needed) to describe model_effort behavior and examples.
tasks.request: Add support for a 'model_effort' field in the queue schema. This field defaults to a value of 'default'. If the runner and model executing the task (currently only codex runner) supports a reasoning effort level the value of 'model_effort' overrides the global and repo config value of reasoning_effort when executing this task. A value of 'default', or if 'model_effort' is excluded, uses the value provided by the config. This enables users to specify variable reasoning efforts based on the task. The task builder should prefer to lean on 'default' but it may choose to set the value to any of the following (all of which are supported by codex, the only runner supporting this at the moment): low, medium (codex default), high, xhigh. If our accepted reasoning levels in ralph differ from these then update them accordingly to match.

tasks.title: Unify runnable task selection for loop/run across CLI and TUI
evidence: Duplication audit: TUI next_loop_task_id re-implements next runnable selection and differs from queue::next_runnable_task and run_cmd::select_run_one_task_index (Doing priority and validation behavior).
plan: Define the intended runnable-task selection policy for loop and run-one (Doing priority, draft handling, validation errors).
Extract or extend a shared selection helper to encode the agreed policy.
Update TUI loop and CLI run selection to call the shared helper.
Add tests for selection ordering, draft handling, and target-id validation errors.
tasks.request: Read .ralph/cache/reports/duplication_and_consistency_audit.md and create tasks in the queue.json. Make sure to add all available fields, use depends_on, etc. Feel free to combine items in the report into single tasks to be executed together if necessary.

tasks.title: Align CLI and TUI search/filter pipeline with shared core
evidence: Duplication audit: CLI supports scope filtering and regex/case-sensitive search while TUI has fixed case-insensitive non-regex search and no scope filter.
plan: Define the shared search/filter semantics and which capabilities are surfaced in CLI vs TUI.
Introduce a shared filter/search core that accepts parameters for regex/case/scope behavior.
Update CLI and TUI to call the shared core with their respective options.
Add tests for regex/case/scope handling and TUI-specific defaults.
tasks.request: Read .ralph/cache/reports/duplication_and_consistency_audit.md and create tasks in the queue.json. Make sure to add all available fields, use depends_on, etc. Feel free to combine items in the report into single tasks to be executed together if necessary.

tasks.title: Consolidate formatting helpers and remove dead helper code
evidence: Duplication audit: formatting helpers duplicated across CLI/TUI; dead helpers exist in queue/operations/mutation.rs and redaction.rs; redundant custom-field formatting in tui/mod.rs.
plan: Identify shared semantic formatting behaviors that can be centralized without changing CLI/TUI presentation.
Consolidate duplicate formatting helpers and remove redundant custom-field formatting logic.
Remove dead helper code with allow(dead_code) where unused, updating tests accordingly.
Add tests for consolidated formatting and ensure removal does not regress existing outputs.

tasks.title: Add TUI flow to create a task from a description
evidence: User request: Add the functional equivalent of running 'ralph task \"Task description here\"' to the TUI.
plan: Locate the TUI command/action flow and identify the proper entry point for task creation.
Implement a TUI action that accepts a task description and invokes the same task creation path as `ralph task`.
Add tests covering successful task creation and validation/error cases for the new TUI action.
tasks.request: Add the functional equivalent of running 'ralph task \"Task description here\"' to the TUI

tasks.title: Add CLI parse tests for TUI entrypoint and interactive flags
evidence: Only a single interactive-related test exists (`cli_rejects_run_one_id_with_interactive`) in `crates/ralph/src/cli/mod.rs`; there are no tests ensuring valid `-i` paths parse correctly.
plan: Add CLI parsing tests for `ralph run one -i` and `ralph run loop -i`.
If a new TUI subcommand is added, add parse tests for it.
Ensure tests cover negative and positive interactive cases.
